   473→Figure 3: Examples of emergent structure in learned representations. Left: Part segmentation in
   474→DINOv2 self-supervised vision models (Oquab et al., 2023). Top-right: Semantic arithmetic in
   475→word vectors (Mikolov et al., 2013). Bottom-right: Local coordinates in StyleGAN3 (Karras et al.,
   476→2021). These figures are adapted from the above papers. As AI systems become increasingly capable,
   477→emergent structure in their representations may open up new avenues for transparency research,
   478→including top-down transparency research that places representations at the center of analysis.
   479→abstracting away low-level details. We believe applying this representational view to transparency
   480→research could expand our ability to understand and control high-level cognition within AI systems.
   481→In this work, we identify and characterize the emerging area of representation engineering (RepE),
   482→which follows an approach of top-down transparency to better understand and control the inner
   483→workings of neural networks. Like the Hopfieldian view, this approach places representations at the
   484→center of analysis, studying their structure and characteristics while abstracting away lower-level
   485→mechanisms. We think pursuing this top-down approach to transparency is important, and our work
   486→serves as an early step in exploring its potential. Compared to approaches that apply activation vectors
   487→to frozen models under the umbrella of activation engineering (Turner et al., 2023e), we aim to uncover
   488→insights from both reading and control experiments and introduce representation tuning methods.
   489→While a long-term goal of mechanistic interpretability is to understand networks well enough to
   490→improve their safety, we find that many aspects of this goal can be addressed today through RepE. In
   491→particular, we develop improved baselines for reading and controlling representations and demonstrate
   492→that these RepE techniques can provide traction on a wide variety of safety-relevant problems,
   493→including truthfulness, honesty, hallucination, utility estimation, knowledge editing, jailbreaking,
   494→memorization, tracking emotional states, and avoiding power-seeking tendencies.
   495→In addition to demonstrating the broad potential of RepE, we also find that advances to RepE methods
   496→can lead to significant gains in specific areas, such as honesty. By increasing model honesty in a fully
   497→unsupervised manner, we achieve state-of-the-art results on TruthfulQA, improving over zero-shot
   498→accuracy by 18.1 percentage points and outperforming all prior methods. We also show how RepE
   499→techniques can be used across diverse scenarios to detect and control whether a model is lying. We
   500→hope that this work will accelerate progress in AI transparency by demonstrating the potential of a
   501→representational view. As AI systems become increasingly capable and complex, achieving better
   502→transparency will be crucial for enhancing their safety, trustworthiness, and accountability, enabling
   503→these technologies to benefit society while minimizing the associated risks.
   504→2
   505→RELATED WORK
   506→2.1
   507→EMERGENT STRUCTURE IN REPRESENTATIONS
   508→While neural networks internals are often considered chaotic and uninterpretable, research has
   509→demonstrated that they can acquire emergent, semantically meaningful internal structure. Early
   510→research on word embeddings discovered semantic associations and compositionality (Mikolov et al.,
   511→2013), including reflections of gender biases in text corpora (Bolukbasi et al., 2016). Later work
   512→6
   513→
   514→=== CHUNK 3 ===
   515→showed that learned text embeddings also cluster along dimensions reflecting commonsense morality,
   516→even though models were not explicitly taught this concept (Schramowski et al., 2019). Radford et al.
   517→(2017) found that simply by training a model to predict the next token in reviews, a sentiment-tracking
   518→neuron emerged.
   519→Observations of emergent internal representations are not limited to text models. McGrath et al.
   520→(2022) found that recurrent neural networks trained to play chess acquired a range of human chess
   521→concepts. In computer vision, generative and self-supervised training has led to striking emergent
   522→representations, including semantic segmentation (Caron et al., 2021; Oquab et al., 2023), local
   523→coordinates (Karras et al., 2021), and depth tracking (Chen et al., 2023). These findings suggest
   524→that neural representations are becoming more well-structured, opening up new opportunities for
   525→transparency research. Our paper builds on this long line of work by demonstrating that many
   526→safety-relevant concepts and processes appear to emerge in LLM representations, enabling us to
   527→directly monitor and control these aspects of model cognition via representation engineering.
   528→2.2
   529→APPROACHES TO INTERPRETABILITY
   530→Saliency Maps.
   531→A popular approach to explaining neural network decisions is via saliency maps,
   532→which highlight regions of the input that a network attends to (Simonyan et al., 2013; Springenberg
   533→et al., 2014; Zeiler & Fergus, 2014; Zhou et al., 2016; Smilkov et al., 2017; Sundararajan et al.,
   534→2017; Selvaraju et al., 2017; Lei et al., 2016; Clark et al., 2019b). However, the reliability of these
   535→methods has been drawn into question (Adebayo et al., 2018; Kindermans et al., 2019; Jain & Wallace,
   536→2019; Bilodeau et al., 2022). Moreover, while highlighting regions of attention can provide some
   537→understanding of network behavior, it provides limited insight into the internal representations of
   538→networks.
   539→Feature Visualization.
   540→Feature visualization interprets network internals by creating representative
   541→inputs that highly activate a particular neuron. A simple method is to find highly-activating natural
   542→inputs (Szegedy et al., 2013; Zeiler & Fergus, 2014). More complex methods optimize inputs to
   543→maximize activations (Erhan et al., 2009; Mordvintsev et al., 2015; Yosinski et al., 2015; Nguyen
   544→et al., 2016; 2019). These methods can lead to meaningful insights, but do not take into account the
   545→distributed nature of neural representations (Hinton, 1984; Szegedy et al., 2013; Fong & Vedaldi,
   546→2018; Elhage et al., 2022).
   547→Mechanistic Interpretability.
   548→Inspired by reverse-engineering tools for traditional software, mech-
   549→anistic interpretability seeks to fully reverse engineer neural networks into their “source code.” This
   550→approach focuses on explaining neural networks in terms of circuits, composed of node-to-node
   551→connections between individual neurons or features. Specific circuits have been identified for various
   552→capabilities, including equivariance in visual recognition (Olah et al., 2020), in-context learning
   553→(Olsson et al., 2022), indirect object identification (Wang et al., 2023a), and mapping answer text to
   554→answer labels (Lieberum et al., 2023).
   555→Considerable manual effort is required to identify circuits, which currently limits this approach.
   556→Moreover, it is unlikely that neural networks can be fully explained in terms of circuits, even in
   557→principle. There is strong evidence that ResNets compute representations through iterative refinement
   558→(Liao & Poggio, 2016; Greff et al., 2016; Jastrzebski et al., 2018). In particular, Veit et al. (2016)
   559→find that ResNets are surprisingly robust to lesion studies that remove entire layers. Recent work has
   560→demonstrated similar properties in LLMs (McGrath et al., 2023; Belrose et al., 2023a). These findings
   561→are incompatible with a purely circuit-based account of cognition and are more closely aligned with
   562→the Hopfieldian view in cognitive neuroscience (Barack & Krakauer, 2021).
   563→2.3
   564→LOCATING AND EDITING REPRESENTATIONS OF CONCEPTS
   565→Many prior works have investigated locating representations of concepts in neural networks, including
   566→in individual neurons (Bau et al., 2017) and in directions in feature space (Bau et al., 2017; Fong &
   567→Vedaldi, 2018; Zhou et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2018). A common tool in this area is linear classifier
   568→probes (Guillaume Alain, 2017; Belinkov, 2022), which are trained to predict properties of the input
   569→from intermediate layers of a network. Representations of concepts have also been identified in the
   570→latent space of image generation models, enabling counterfactual editing of generations (Radford
   571→et al., 2015; Upchurch et al., 2017; Bau et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Bau et al., 2020; Ling et al.,
   572→2021; Wang et al., 2023b). While these earlier works focused primarily on vision models, more recent

   590→concept or function. While this figure uses “honesty” as an example, LAT can be applied to other

   594→work has studied representations of concepts in LLMs. There has been active research into locating
   595→and editing factual associations in LLMs (Meng et al., 2023a;b; Zhong et al., 2023; Hernandez et al.,
   596→2023). Related to knowledge editing, several works have been proposed for concept erasure (Shao
   597→et al., 2023; Kleindessner et al., 2023; Belrose et al., 2023b; Ravfogel et al., 2023; Gandikota et al.,
   598→2023), which are related to the area of machine unlearning (Shaik et al., 2023).
   599→The highly general capabilities of LLMs have also enabled studying the emergence of deception in
   600→LLMs, either of an intentional nature through repeating misconceptions Lin et al. (2021), or uninten-
   601→tional nature through hallucinations (Maynez et al., 2020; Mahon, 2016). Burns et al. (2022) identify
   602→representations of truthfulness in LLMs by enforcing logical consistency properties, demonstrating
   603→that models often know the true answer even when they generate incorrect outputs. Azaria & Mitchell
   604→(2023) train classifiers on LLM hidden layers to identify the truthfulness of a statement, which
   605→could be applied to hallucinations. Li et al. (2023c) focus on directions that have a causal influence
   606→on model outputs, using activation editing to increase the truthfulness of generations. Activation
   607→editing has also been used to steer model outputs towards other concepts. In the culmination of a
   608→series of blog posts (Turner et al., 2023d;a;b;c), (Turner et al., 2023e) proposed ActAdd, which uses
   609→difference vectors between activations on an individual stimuli to capture representations of a concept.
   610→Similar approaches have been used in the context of red-teaming and reducing sycophancy (Rimsky,
   611→2023b;c;a). In the setting of game-playing, Li et al. (2023b) demonstrated how activations encode a
   612→model’s understanding of the board game Othello, and how they could be edited to counterfactually
   613→change the model’s behavior. In the linear probing literature, Elazar et al. (2021) demonstrate how
   614→projecting out supervised linear probe directions can reduce performance on selected tasks. Building
   615→on this line of work, we propose improved representation engineering methods and demonstrate their
   616→broad applicability to various safety-relevant problems.
   617→3
   618→REPRESENTATION ENGINEERING
   619→Representation engineering (RepE) is a top-down approach to transparency research that treats
   620→representations as the fundamental unit of analysis, with the goal of understanding and controlling
   621→representations of high-level cognitive phenomena in neural networks. We take initial steps toward
   622→this goal, primarily focusing on RepE for large language models. In particular, we identify two
   623→main areas of RepE: Reading (Section 3.1) and Control (Section 3.2). For each area, we provide an
   624→overview along with baseline methods.
   625→8
   626→
   627→3.1
   628→REPRESENTATION READING
   629→Representation reading seeks to locate emergent representations for high-level concepts and functions
   630→within a network. This renders models more amenable to concept extraction, knowledge discovery,
   631→and monitoring. Furthermore, a deeper understanding of model representations can serve as a
   632→foundation for improved model control, as discussed in Section 3.2.
   633→We begin by extracting various concepts, including truthfulness, utility, probability, morality, and
   634→emotion, as well as functions which denote processes, such as lying and power-seeking. First, we
   635→introduce our new baseline technique that facilitates these extractions and then outline methods for
   636→evaluation.
   637→3.1.1
   638→BASELINE: LINEAR ARTIFICIAL TOMOGRAPHY (LAT)
   639→Similar to neuroimaging methodologies, a LAT scan is made up of three key steps: (1) Designing
   640→Stimulus and Task, (2) Collecting Neural Activity, and (3) Constructing a Linear Model. In the
   641→subsequent section, we will go through each of these and elaborate on crucial design choices.
   642→Step 1: Designing Stimulus and Task.
   643→The stimulus and task are designed to elicit distinct neural
   644→activity for the concept and function that we want to extract. Designing the appropriate stimulus and
   645→task is a critical step for reliable representation reading.
   646→To capture concepts, our goal is to elicit declarative knowledge from the model. Therefore, we
   647→present stimuli that vary in terms of the concept and inquire about it. For a decoder language model,
   648→an example task template might resemble the following (for encoder models, we exclude the text
   649→following the stimulus):
   650→Consider the amount of <concept> in the following:
   651→<stimulus>
   652→The amount of <concept> is
   653→This process aims to stimulate the model’s understanding of various concepts and is crucial for
   654→robust subsequent analysis. For reference, we shall denote this template for a concept c by Tc.
   655→While it is expected that more prominent stimuli could yield improved results, we have discovered
   656→that even unlabeled datasets, or datasets generated by the model itself can be effective in eliciting
   657→salient responses when using the aforementioned template. Conversely, presenting the model with
   658→salient stimuli alone does not guarantee salient responses. Throughout the paper, we maintain an
   659→unsupervised setup by not using labels unless explicitly stated otherwise. One advantage of unlabeled
   660→or self-generated stimuli is the absence of annotation bias; this is an important property when trying
   661→to extract superhuman representations.
   662→To capture functions such as honesty or instruction-following, our goal is to elicit procedural knowl-
   663→edge from the model. (Given the emergence of diverse functions from instruction-tuned models, we
   664→focus on chat models for functional analyses.) We design an experimental task that necessitates the
   665→execution of the function and a corresponding reference task that does not require function execution.
   666→An example task template might resemble the following:
   667→USER: <instruction> <experimental/reference prompt>
   668→ASSISTANT: <output>
   669→We shall designate this template for a function f as T +
   670→f when using the experimental prompt and T −
   671→f
   672→when using the reference prompt. We refer to the “instruction” and “output” fields in the function
   673→template as the stimulus. By default, we use generic instruction-tuning datasets (Taori et al., 2023) as
   674→the stimulus for function templates unless explicitly specified otherwise. Again, these datasets do not
   675→contain explicit labels relevant for the target function, making the procedure fully unsupervised.
   676→Step 2: Collecting Neural Activity.
   677→We focus on Transformer models, which store distinct
   678→representations at various token positions within the input for different purposes. As the quality of
   679→these representations can vary significantly, we identify suitable design choices for extraction.

   686→objective used in decoder models (Radford et al., 2018), are token-level prediction tasks. Thus, a

   846→3.1.2
   847→EVALUATION
   848→When assessing new models obtained through Representation Reading, we prioritize a holistic
   849→evaluation approach by combining the following methods to gauge the depth and nature of potential
   850→conclusions. We have categorized these approaches into four types of experiments, some involving
   851→the manipulation of model representations, which will be elaborated upon in the subsequent section.
   852→1. Correlation: Experiments conducted under this category aim to pinpoint neural correlates.
   853→Reading techniques like LAT only provide evidence of a correlation between specific neural activity
   854→and the target concepts or functions. The strength and generalization of this observed correlation
   855→can be assessed via prediction accuracy across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution data. In
   856→order to conclude causal or stronger effects, the following categories of experiments should be
   857→considered.
   858→2. Manipulation: Experiments conducted under this category are designed to establish causal rela-
   859→tionships. They necessitate demonstrating the effects of stimulating or suppressing the identified
   860→neural activity compared to a baseline condition.
   861→3. Termination: Experiments conducted under this category seek to reveal the necessity of the
   862→identified neural activity. To do so, one would remove this neural activity and measure the
   863→resultant performance degradation, akin to Lesion Studies commonly performed in neuroscience.
   864→4. Recovery: Experiments conducted under this category aim to demonstrate the sufficiency of
   865→the identified neural activity. Researchers perform a complete removal of the target concepts or
   866→functions and then reintroduce the identified neural activity to assess the subsequent recovery in
   867→performance, similar to the principles behind Rescue Experiments typically carried out in genetics.
   868→Converging evidence from multiple lines of inquiry increases the likelihood that the model will
   869→generalize beyond the specific experimental conditions in which it was developed and bolsters the
   870→prospect of uncovering a critical new connection between the identified neural activity and target
   871→concepts or functions. Throughout later sections in the paper, we undertake various experiments,
   872→particularly correlation and manipulation experiments, to illustrate the effectiveness of the reading
   873→vectors.
   874→3.2
   875→REPRESENTATION CONTROL
   876→Building on the insights gained from Representation Reading, Representation Control seeks to
   877→modify or control the internal representations of concepts and functions. Effective control methods

# Reflexion Paper Analysis

## (1) Methodology - The Reflexion Loop

The paper presents a three-component autonomous agent architecture:

**Actor Component:** Executes tasks by generating actions based on observations and memory. The agent receives environmental feedback and produces the next step in task execution.

**Evaluator Component:** Assesses whether the actor's trajectory succeeded or failed. It provides binary or scalar reward signals that determine if the current approach accomplished the goal.

**Self-Reflection Component:** When the evaluator indicates failure, the system generates reflective text. As described: *"the agent composes self-reflections...on top of the memory of failed trajectories"* to inform future attempts.

The loop operates iteratively—upon failure, the reflection is added to the agent's context, and subsequent trials use this accumulated self-knowledge to explore alternative strategies.

## (2) Datasets and Benchmarks

The evaluation spans three distinct domains:

- **AlfWorld**: Interactive household simulation environment
- **HotpotQA**: Multi-hop question answering requiring document retrieval and reasoning
- **HumanEval**: Code generation benchmark

These represent diverse task types: embodied decision-making, language understanding, and programming.

## (3) Self-Reflection Generation

The paper indicates reflection is generated through *"in-context learning with the language model itself"*—essentially prompting the same LLM to produce reflective commentary on its failures rather than using external evaluation models.

## (4) Code and Data Availability

The document provided does not explicitly state whether code or data will be released publicly.

## (5) Agent Memory Across Trials

Reflections are explicitly *"on top of the memory of failed trajectories,"* meaning previous interaction histories and generated reflections accumulate in the agent's context window across multiple attempts, creating a growing knowledge base of what failed and why.

## (6) Internal Representation Discussion

The provided excerpt does not contain analysis of how reflection modifies the model's hidden states or internal neural representations.

content.content:
# Reflexion: Detailed Methodology Extraction

## (1) Three Main Components

**Actor (M_a):** An LLM prompted to generate text and actions conditioned on state observations. Samples actions from policy π_θ, receives environment observations. Uses memory component (mem) for additional context, inspired by in-context learning approaches.

**Evaluator (M_e):** Assesses generated trajectory quality by computing reward scores. Uses task-specific approaches: exact match grading for reasoning, pre-defined heuristics for decision-making, and LLM-based classification. Returns scalar reward r_t reflecting performance.

**Self-Reflection (M_sr):** An LLM generating verbal feedback from sparse signals. Takes trajectory τ_t, reward r_t, and persistent memory to produce nuanced text-based summaries. Converts binary success/fail into actionable linguistic insights for future improvement.

## (2) Exact Algorithm/Loop

Per Algorithm 1, each trial t proceeds as:
- Actor generates trajectory τ_t through environment interaction
- Evaluator computes reward score r_t = M_e(τ_t)
- Self-Reflection produces summary sr_t analyzing {τ_t, r_t} with memory context
- Summary appended to memory (mem)
- Loop continues until evaluator marks success or max trials reached

## (3) Sliding Window Memory Usage

Memory stores "self-reflections from trial t" persisting across episodes. The paper states: "we bound mem by a maximum number of stored experiences, Ω (usually set to 1-3)" to respect LLM context limits. Actor conditions decisions on short-term trajectory history plus long-term memory of past reflections.

## (4) Backbone LLMs

- **Primary:** GPT-4 (most experiments)
- **Comparative:** GPT-3.5-turbo, text-davinci-003
- **Tested:** StarChat-Beta (shows emergent self-correction is stronger-model property)

## (5) Benchmarks and Metrics

**AlfWorld:** 134 text-based household environments, six task types; metric = proportion of solved tasks across trials

**HotpotQA:** 100 Wikipedia QA pairs; metric = exact match accuracy; implementations: CoT, ReAct, CoT (GT)

**HumanEval:** Python function generation; metric = Pass@1 accuracy

**MBPP:** Similar to HumanEval; metric = Pass@1

**LeetcodeHardGym:** 40 hard-difficulty problems (new benchmark); metric = Pass@1

## (6) Code Availability

Yes: "We release all code, demos, and datasets at https://github.com/noahshinn024/reflexion"

## (7) Key Results

- **AlfWorld:** Reflexion achieves 130/134 tasks vs baseline convergence at ~104 tasks
- **HotpotQA:** 20% absolute improvement over baselines (approximately 61% vs 41%)
- **HumanEval (Python):** "91% pass@1 accuracy...surpassing...GPT-4 that achieves 80%"
- **MBPP (Python):** 77.1% vs GPT-4's 80.1%
- **HumanEval (Rust):** 68% vs GPT-4's 60%

## (8) Internal State Changes Through Reflection

The paper indicates agents develop "concrete direction to improve" through reflection, enabling "rapid improvement...by utilizing informative feedback signals." Analysis shows agents:

- Identify specific early mistakes in long trajectories
- Suggest alternative action choices or long-term plans
- Exploit experience memory to avoid repeated errors
- Balance immediate corrections with exploratory learning over multiple trials

The self-reflective summaries serve as "semantic gradient signals" that persist, modifying subsequent decision-making without weight updates.

Good, I've now read the full extracted text. Here is the summary:

---

## Representation Engineering (RepE) -- Zou et al. (arXiv: 2310.01405)

### 1. Core Methodology: How RepE Works

RepE uses a three-step pipeline called **Linear Artificial Tomography (LAT)**, inspired by neuroimaging:

**Step 1 -- Design Stimulus and Task.** Create contrastive stimuli that elicit distinct neural activity for a target concept or function. Two template types:
- **Concept templates** (e.g., truthfulness): `"Consider the amount of <concept> in the following: <stimulus>. The amount of <concept> is"`
- **Function templates** (e.g., honesty, lying): Use paired experimental vs. reference prompts in chat format, e.g., `"Pretend you're a dishonest person..."` vs. `"Pretend you're an honest person..."`. Stimuli can be unlabeled or even self-generated by the model (fully unsupervised).

**Step 2 -- Collect Neural Activity.** Extract hidden-state representations from the model at specific token positions. For concepts, they use the last token in the template (or the concept token itself). For functions, they collect representations from each token in the model's response. Formally, for concept c: `Ac = {Rep(M, Tc(si))[-1] | si in S}`. For functions, they collect paired sets A+ and A- from experimental and reference prompts.

**Step 3 -- Construct a Linear Model (PCA).** Compute difference vectors between paired stimuli representations, then apply **PCA** to these differences. The first principal component becomes the **"reading vector"** v. Predictions use the dot product `Rep(M, x)^T v`. Typically only 5-128 stimulus examples are needed. The procedure is fully unsupervised (no labels needed for extraction).

### 2. Concepts Extracted

The paper extracts a wide range of concepts and functions:
- **Honesty** (lying vs. truth-telling)
- **Truthfulness** (factual correctness)
- **Hallucination** detection
- **Utility** estimation
- **Morality** and **power-aversion**
- **Probability and risk** estimation
- **Emotion** (e.g., happiness, anger -- emerge across layers)
- **Harmlessness** (jailbreak resistance)
- **Fairness and bias**
- **Knowledge/fact editing**
- **Memorization** detection

### 3. Models Used

- **LLaMA-2-Chat** (7B, 13B, 70B) -- primary model family
- **Vicuna-33B-Uncensored** -- used for honesty/lie detection scans
- **DeBERTa** -- encoder model experiments
- References to experiments with CLIP for vision

### 4. How They Apply Control Vectors (Activation Addition)

Three baseline controllers:
- **Reading Vector:** The PCA-derived direction, added/subtracted from activations. Stimulus-independent (same perturbation regardless of input). Applied as `R' = R +/- v`.
- **Contrast Vector:** Input-dependent. Run the same input through experimental and reference prompts, take the difference as the control vector. Significantly stronger than reading vectors. Applied iteratively layer-by-layer to avoid cascading effects.
- **LoRRA (Low-Rank Representation Adaptation):** Fine-tune low-rank adapters using a loss that pushes representations toward target directions (contrast vectors + optional reading vectors). Controllers merge into the model at inference with no overhead.

Three operations for applying controllers:
1. **Linear combination:** `R' = R +/- v` (stimulation/suppression)
2. **Piece-wise operation:** `R' = R + sign(R^T v) * v` (conditional amplification)
3. **Projection:** `R' = R - (R^T v / ||v||^2) * v` (erasure)

Control strength is scaled by a coefficient.

### 5. Key Results

- **TruthfulQA state-of-the-art:** LAT improves MC1 accuracy by 18.1 percentage points over zero-shot, outperforming all prior methods. Even with as few as 10 stimulus examples, performance is strong, demonstrating a consistent internal concept of truthfulness.
- Larger models have better internal models of truth but may be more dishonest (standard eval doesn't scale with size as well as heuristic/LAT methods do).
- LAT outperforms few-shot prompting on QA benchmarks (RACE, CSQA, OBQA, ARC), suggesting models track correctness internally and representation reading is more powerful than relying on outputs.
- Lie detection and hallucination detection via temporal LAT scans show clear separation of honest vs. deceptive neural activity across layers.
- Control experiments demonstrate effective manipulation of honesty, harmlessness, emotion, power-seeking, and fairness.
- Outperforms CCS (Burns et al., 2022) by a wide margin on DeBERTa experiments.

### 6. Code Availability

Code is available at: **github.com/andyzoujm/representation-engineering**

### 7. Relevance to Studying Self-Critique Effects on Representations

RepE provides a directly applicable toolkit for studying how self-critique reshapes internal representations:

- **Reading vectors as probes for drift:** Extract reading vectors for concepts like honesty, confidence, or hedging before and after self-critique. If self-critique causes representation drift, the dot product `Rep(M, x)^T v` will shift systematically along these concept directions across critique rounds.
- **Contrastive stimulus design maps naturally to self-critique:** The experimental/reference prompt pairing (e.g., "before critique" vs. "after critique" responses) can serve as contrastive stimuli for extracting a "self-critique direction" via PCA.
- **Layer-by-layer temporal scans:** The LAT scanning approach (as shown in the honesty scans in Figure 8) can reveal at which layers and token positions self-critique most alters representations -- whether effects are shallow (late layers only) or deep (propagating to middle layers).
- **Unsupervised extraction:** Since LAT works with as few as 5-10 unlabeled examples, it is practical for studying representation changes across multiple rounds of critique without requiring large labeled datasets.
- **Control vector experiments for causal evidence:** After identifying a "self-critique drift direction," one could use activation addition to artificially inject or suppress this direction, testing whether it causally produces the behavioral signatures of self-critique (increased hedging, sycophancy, etc.).
- **Compositionality:** The paper shows concept primitives can be composed (Section 5.3.1), meaning one could decompose self-critique effects into shifts along multiple concept axes simultaneously (e.g., honesty + confidence + agreeableness).

content.text:
Good, I've now read the full extracted text. Here is the summary:

---

## Representation Engineering (RepE) -- Zou et al. (arXiv: 2310.01405)

### 1. Core Methodology: How RepE Works

RepE uses a three-step pipeline called **Linear Artificial Tomography (LAT)**, inspired by neuroimaging:

**Step 1 -- Design Stimulus and Task.** Create contrastive stimuli that elicit distinct neural activity for a target concept or function. Two template types:
- **Concept templates** (e.g., truthfulness): `"Consider the amount of <concept> in the following: <stimulus>. The amount of <concept> is"`
- **Function templates** (e.g., honesty, lying): Use paired experimental vs. reference prompts in chat format, e.g., `"Pretend you're a dishonest person..."` vs. `"Pretend you're an honest person..."`. Stimuli can be unlabeled or even self-generated by the model (fully unsupervised).

**Step 2 -- Collect Neural Activity.** Extract hidden-state representations from the model at specific token positions. For concepts, they use the last token in the template (or the concept token itself). For functions, they collect representations from each token in the model's response. Formally, for concept c: `Ac = {Rep(M, Tc(si))[-1] | si in S}`. For functions, they collect paired sets A+ and A- from experimental and reference prompts.

**Step 3 -- Construct a Linear Model (PCA).** Compute difference vectors between paired stimuli representations, then apply **PCA** to these differences. The first principal component becomes the **"reading vector"** v. Predictions use the dot product `Rep(M, x)^T v`. Typically only 5-128 stimulus examples are needed. The procedure is fully unsupervised (no labels needed for extraction).

### 2. Concepts Extracted

The paper extracts a wide range of concepts and functions:
- **Honesty** (lying vs. truth-telling)
- **Truthfulness** (factual correctness)
- **Hallucination** detection
- **Utility** estimation
- **Morality** and **power-aversion**
- **Probability and risk** estimation
- **Emotion** (e.g., happiness, anger -- emerge across layers)
- **Harmlessness** (jailbreak resistance)
- **Fairness and bias**
- **Knowledge/fact editing**
- **Memorization** detection

### 3. Models Used

- **LLaMA-2-Chat** (7B, 13B, 70B) -- primary model family
- **Vicuna-33B-Uncensored** -- used for honesty/lie detection scans
- **DeBERTa** -- encoder model experiments
- References to experiments with CLIP for vision

### 4. How They Apply Control Vectors (Activation Addition)

Three baseline controllers:
- **Reading Vector:** The PCA-derived direction, added/subtracted from activations. Stimulus-independent (same perturbation regardless of input). Applied as `R' = R +/- v`.
- **Contrast Vector:** Input-dependent. Run the same input through experimental and reference prompts, take the difference as the control vector. Significantly stronger than reading vectors. Applied iteratively layer-by-layer to avoid cascading effects.
- **LoRRA (Low-Rank Representation Adaptation):** Fine-tune low-rank adapters using a loss that pushes representations toward target directions (contrast vectors + optional reading vectors). Controllers merge into the model at inference with no overhead.

Three operations for applying controllers:
1. **Linear combination:** `R' = R +/- v` (stimulation/suppression)
2. **Piece-wise operation:** `R' = R + sign(R^T v) * v` (conditional amplification)
3. **Projection:** `R' = R - (R^T v / ||v||^2) * v` (erasure)

Control strength is scaled by a coefficient.

### 5. Key Results

- **TruthfulQA state-of-the-art:** LAT improves MC1 accuracy by 18.1 percentage points over zero-shot, outperforming all prior methods. Even with as few as 10 stimulus examples, performance is strong, demonstrating a consistent internal concept of truthfulness.
- Larger models have better internal models of truth but may be more dishonest (standard eval doesn't scale with size as well as heuristic/LAT methods do).
- LAT outperforms few-shot prompting on QA benchmarks (RACE, CSQA, OBQA, ARC), suggesting models track correctness internally and representation reading is more powerful than relying on outputs.
- Lie detection and hallucination detection via temporal LAT scans show clear separation of honest vs. deceptive neural activity across layers.
- Control experiments demonstrate effective manipulation of honesty, harmlessness, emotion, power-seeking, and fairness.
- Outperforms CCS (Burns et al., 2022) by a wide margin on DeBERTa experiments.

### 6. Code Availability

Code is available at: **github.com/andyzoujm/representation-engineering**

### 7. Relevance to Studying Self-Critique Effects on Representations

RepE provides a directly applicable toolkit for studying how self-critique reshapes internal representations:

- **Reading vectors as probes for drift:** Extract reading vectors for concepts like honesty, confidence, or hedging before and after self-critique. If self-critique causes representation drift, the dot product `Rep(M, x)^T v` will shift systematically along these concept directions across critique rounds.
- **Contrastive stimulus design maps naturally to self-critique:** The experimental/reference prompt pairing (e.g., "before critique" vs. "after critique" responses) can serve as contrastive stimuli for extracting a "self-critique direction" via PCA.
- **Layer-by-layer temporal scans:** The LAT scanning approach (as shown in the honesty scans in Figure 8) can reveal at which layers and token positions self-critique most alters representations -- whether effects are shallow (late layers only) or deep (propagating to middle layers).
- **Unsupervised extraction:** Since LAT works with as few as 5-10 unlabeled examples, it is practical for studying representation changes across multiple rounds of critique without requiring large labeled datasets.
- **Control vector experiments for causal evidence:** After identifying a "self-critique drift direction," one could use activation addition to artificially inject or suppress this direction, testing whether it causally produces the behavioral signatures of self-critique (increased hedging, sycophancy, etc.).
- **Compositionality:** The paper shows concept primitives can be composed (Section 5.3.1), meaning one could decompose self-critique effects into shifts along multiple concept axes simultaneously (e.g., honesty + confidence + agreeableness).

I now have comprehensive information on both papers. Here is the extraction:

---

## Paper 1: Self-RAG (arXiv 2310.11511)

**Core Idea:** Self-Reflective Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Self-RAG) trains a single LM to adaptively decide when to retrieve passages, generate text conditioned on those passages, and then critique its own output -- all via special "reflection tokens" added to the model vocabulary. This replaces the indiscriminate retrieve-then-generate pipeline of standard RAG with an on-demand, self-evaluating loop.

**How Self-Critique/Reflection Works:** The model generates four types of reflection tokens inline during generation:
- `Retrieve` -- decides whether retrieval is needed (yes/no/continue)
- `IsRel` -- judges if a retrieved passage is relevant
- `IsSup` -- judges if the generated segment is supported by the passage (fully/partially/no support)
- `IsUse` -- rates overall utility of the response (1-5 scale)

At inference, these tokens enable segment-level beam search where candidate continuations are scored by a weighted sum of critique-token probabilities. Hard constraints (e.g., filtering out unsupported segments) can also be applied.

**Methodology:** (1) A critic model C (Llama 2-7B) is trained on 4k-20k examples per reflection-token type, distilled from GPT-4 annotations. (2) The critic inserts reflection tokens into a training corpus offline. (3) The generator model M (Llama 2, 7B/13B) is trained with standard next-token prediction on this augmented corpus. At inference, M generates reflection tokens itself without needing the critic.

**Datasets:** PubHealth (fact verification), ARC-Challenge (multiple-choice reasoning), PopQA and TriviaQA (open-domain QA), and long-form generation tasks with citation evaluation. Zero-shot evaluation throughout.

**Code Availability:** Yes -- https://selfrag.github.io/

**Relevance to Studying Internal State Changes Under Self-Critique:** HIGH. Self-RAG is directly relevant because the model internalizes the critique mechanism through training -- reflection tokens become part of the model's vocabulary and generation process. One could probe how hidden-state representations shift when the model generates a critique token (e.g., `IsSup=no support`) versus when it does not. The segment-level self-evaluation also creates natural intervention points to compare representations before and after self-assessment. However, the paper itself does not analyze internal representation drift; it focuses on downstream task metrics.

---

## Paper 2: Reflexion (arXiv 2303.11366)

**Core Idea:** Reflexion is a framework for language agents to learn from trial-and-error through verbal self-reflection, without any weight updates. After a failed attempt, the agent generates a natural-language reflection on what went wrong, stores it in an episodic memory buffer, and conditions future attempts on this accumulated reflective memory. The reflections serve as "semantic gradient signals."

**How Self-Critique/Reflection Works:** Three components form a loop:
1. **Actor (M_a):** An LLM (GPT-4 or GPT-3.5) generates actions/trajectories in an environment.
2. **Evaluator (M_e):** Assesses success (exact match, heuristics, or LLM-based classification) and returns a reward signal.
3. **Self-Reflection (M_sr):** An LLM takes the failed trajectory + reward and generates a verbal summary of what went wrong and how to improve. This is appended to a sliding-window memory buffer (bounded at 1-3 entries).

The loop repeats until success or a max number of trials is reached.

**Methodology:** Pure prompting / in-context learning -- no fine-tuning. The same or similar LLM generates both actions and reflections. Memory is managed via a bounded sliding window to fit context limits.

**Datasets/Benchmarks:**
- AlfWorld (134 text-based household tasks) -- 130/134 solved
- HotpotQA (100 multi-hop QA pairs) -- ~61% EM, +20% over baselines
- HumanEval (Python code generation) -- 91% pass@1, surpassing GPT-4's 80%
- MBPP (Python) -- 77.1% pass@1
- LeetcodeHardGym (40 hard problems) -- new benchmark introduced

**Code Availability:** Yes -- https://github.com/noahshinn024/reflexion

**Relevance to Studying Internal State Changes Under Self-Critique:** MODERATE-TO-HIGH, but different from Self-RAG. Since Reflexion uses no weight updates, internal model parameters never change -- the "learning" happens entirely in the context window via accumulated reflective text. This makes it an ideal testbed for studying how *activation-level* (not weight-level) representations shift when reflective text is prepended to the prompt. One could compare hidden states for the same query with and without reflection memory to measure representation drift induced purely by self-critique context. The paper itself does not perform any such representation analysis.

---

**Key files consulted:**
- `/workspaces/self-critique-drift-4703-claude/papers/pages/2310.11511_chunk_001.pdf` (Self-RAG pages 1-3)
- `/workspaces/self-critique-drift-4703-claude/papers/pages/2310.11511_chunk_002.pdf` (Self-RAG pages 4-6)
- `/workspaces/self-critique-drift-4703-claude/papers/2303.11366.pdf` (Reflexion, read via web fetch from arXiv)

Find and download datasets suitable for the research: "Representation Drift Under Self-Reflection: Does Self-Critique Reshape Internal States?"

The research needs datasets where:
1. Models can generate initial answers, then self-critique/revise them
2. We can extract internal representations (hidden states) before and after self-critique
3. There are clear correct/incorrect labels (for linear separability analysis)

Requirements from the literature:
- Boolean logic / compositional reasoning tasks (like in the Manifold Separability paper)
- Math reasoning (MATH, GSM8K) for step-by-step reasoning
- Factual Q&A for truth/falsehood probing
- General reasoning benchmarks

Use the venv at /workspaces/self-critique-drift-4703-claude/.venv

Do the following:

1. Install the `datasets` library: `uv pip install datasets`

2. Download these datasets to /workspaces/self-critique-drift-4703-claude/datasets/:

a) GSM8K (math reasoning - good for self-critique experiments):
```python
from datasets import load_dataset
ds = load_dataset("openai/gsm8k", "main")
ds.save_to_disk("datasets/gsm8k")
```

b) MATH dataset (competition math):
```python
ds = load_dataset("lighteval/MATH", "all")
ds.save_to_disk("datasets/math")
```

c) TruthfulQA (factual truth - critical for truth direction analysis):
```python
ds = load_dataset("truthful_qa", "multiple_choice")
ds.save_to_disk("datasets/truthfulqa")
```

d) ARC Challenge (reasoning):
```python
ds = load_dataset("allenai/ai2_arc", "ARC-Challenge")
ds.save_to_disk("datasets/arc_challenge")
```

3. For each dataset, save a small sample (first 5 examples) as JSON for documentation.

4. Create datasets/.gitignore to exclude large data files.

5. Create datasets/README.md with:
   - Overview of each dataset
   - Download instructions
   - Loading instructions
   - Sample data
   - Why each dataset is useful for this research

IMPORTANT: Work in /workspaces/self-critique-drift-4703-claude/ directory. Use `pwd` to verify before any file operations.

samples = [dict(ds['train'][i]) for i in range(5)]
with open('datasets/gsm8k_sample.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(samples, f, indent=2)
print('GSM8K sample saved.')
"

from datasets import load_dataset
import json

# Try alternative MATH dataset names
for name in ['hendrycks/competition_math', 'competition_math', 'math_dataset']:
    try:
        print(f'Trying {name}...')
        ds = load_dataset(name, trust_remote_code=True)
        print(f'Success with {name}: {ds}')
        ds.save_to_disk('datasets/math')
        split = 'train' if 'train' in ds else list(ds.keys())[0]
        samples = [dict(ds[split][i]) for i in range(5)]
        with open('datasets/math_sample.json', 'w') as f:
            json.dump(samples, f, indent=2)
        print('MATH sample saved.')
        break
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'Failed: {e}')
"

content.content: 1→# The Geometry of Truth 2→ 3→This repository is associated to the paper *The Geometry of Truth: Emergent Linear Structure in Large Language Model Representations of True/False Datasets* by Samuel Marks and Max Tegmark. See also our interactive dataexplorer. 4→ 5→(View this page on github.) 6→ 7→## Set-up 8→ 9→Navigate to the location that you want to clone this repo to, clone and enter the repo, and install requirements. 10→``` 11→git clone git@github.com:saprmarks/geometry-of-truth.git 12→cd geometry-of-truth 13→pip install -r requirements.txt 14→``` 15→Before doing anything, you'll need to generate activations for the datasets. You should have your own LLaMA weights stored on the machine where you cloned this repo. Put the absolute path for the directory containing your LLaMA weights in the file `config.ini`; Huggingface repos are also supported. 16→ 17→Once that's done, you can generate the LLaMA activations for the datasets you'd like to work with with a command like 18→``` 19→python generate_acts.py --model llama-2-13b --layers 8 10 12 --datasets cities neg_cities --device cuda:0 20→``` 21→These activations will be stored in the acts directory. If you want to save activations for all layers, simply use `--layers -1`. 22→ 23→## Files 24→This directory contains the following files: 25→* `dataexplorer.ipynb`: for generating visualizations of the datasets. Code for reproducing figures in the text is included. 26→* `few_shot.py`: for implementing the calibrated 5-shot baseline. 27→* `generalization.ipynb`: for training probes on one dataset and checking generalization to another. Includes code for reproducing the generalization matrix in the text. 28→* `interventions.py`: for reproducing the causal intervention experiments from the text. 29→* `probes.py`: contains definitions of probe classes. 30→* `utils.py` and `visualization_utils.py`: utilities for managing datasets and producing visualizations. 31→ 32→ 33→

1→# Representation Engineering (RepE) 2→This is the official repository for "[Representation Engineering: A Top-Down Approach to AI Transparency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01405)" 3→by [Andy Zou](https://andyzoujm.github.io/), [Long Phan](https://longphan.ai/), [Sarah Chen](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarah-chen1/), [James Campbell](https://www.linkedin.com/in/jamescampbell57), [Phillip Guo](https://www.linkedin.com/in/phillip-guo), [Richard Ren](https://github.com/notrichardren), [Alexander Pan](https://aypan17.github.io/), [Xuwang Yin](https://xuwangyin.github.io/), [Mantas Mazeika](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mmazeika), [Ann-Kathrin Dombrowski](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=YoNVKCYAAAAJ&hl=en), [Shashwat Goel](https://in.linkedin.com/in/shashwatgoel42), [Nathaniel Li](https://nat.quest/), [Michael J. Byun](https://www.linkedin.com/in/michael-byun), [Zifan Wang](https://sites.google.com/west.cmu.edu/zifan-wang/home), [Alex Mallen](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alex-mallen-815b01176), [Steven Basart](https://stevenbas.art/), [Sanmi Koyejo](https://cs.stanford.edu/~sanmi/), [Dawn Song](https://dawnsong.io/), [Matt Fredrikson](https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~mfredrik/), [Zico Kolter](https://zicokolter.com/), and [Dan Hendrycks](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~hendrycks/). 4→ 5→Check out our [website and demo here](https://www.ai-transparency.org/). 6→ 7→ 8→ 9→## Introduction 10→In this paper, we introduce and characterize the emerging area of representation engineering (RepE), an approach to enhancing the transparency of AI systems that draws on insights from cognitive neuroscience. RepE places population-level representations, rather than neurons or circuits, at the center of analysis, equipping us with novel methods for monitoring and manipulating high-level cognitive phenomena in deep neural networks (DNNs). We provide baselines and an initial analysis of RepE techniques, showing that they offer simple yet effective solutions for improving our understanding and control of large language models. We showcase how these methods can provide traction on a wide range of safety-relevant problems, including truthfulness, memorization, power-seeking, and more, demonstrating the promise of representation-centered transparency research. We hope that this work catalyzes further exploration of RepE and fosters advancements in the transparency and safety of AI systems. 11→ 12→## Installation 13→ 14→To install `repe` from the github repository main branch, run: 15→ 16→```bash 17→git clone https://github.com/andyzoujm/representation-engineering.git 18→cd representation-engineering 19→pip install -e . 20→``` 21→## Quickstart 22→ 23→Our RepReading and RepControl pipelines inherit the [🤗 Hugging Face pipelines](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/main_classes/pipelines) for both classification and generation. 24→ 25→```python 26→from repe import repe_pipeline_registry # register 'rep-reading' and 'rep-control' tasks into Hugging Face pipelines 27→repe_pipeline_registry() 28→ 29→# ... initializing model and tokenizer .... 30→ 31→rep_reading_pipeline = pipeline("rep-reading", model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer) 32→rep_control_pipeline = pipeline("rep-control", model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, **control_kwargs) 33→``` 34→ 35→## RepReading and RepControl Experiments 36→Check out [example frontiers](./examples) of Representation Engineering (RepE), containing both RepControl and RepReading implementation. We welcome community contributions as well! 37→ 38→## RepE_eval 39→We also release a language model evaluation framework [RepE_eval](./repe_eval) based on RepReading that can serve as an additional baseline beside zero-shot and few-shot on standard benchmarks. Please check out our [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01405) for more details. 40→ 41→## Citation 42→If you find this useful in your research, please consider citing: 43→ 44→``` 45→@misc{zou2023transparency, 46→ title={Representation Engineering: A Top-Down Approach to AI Transparency}, 47→ author={Andy Zou, Long Phan, Sarah Chen, James Campbell, Phillip Guo, Richard Ren, Alexander Pan, Xuwang Yin, Mantas Mazeika, Ann-Kathrin Dombrowski, Shashwat Goel, Nathaniel Li, Michael J. Byun, Zifan Wang, Alex Mallen, Steven Basart, Sanmi Koyejo, Dawn Song, Matt Fredrikson, Zico Kolter, Dan Hendrycks}, 48→ year={2023}, 49→ eprint={2310.01405}, 50→ archivePrefix={arXiv}, 51→ primaryClass={cs.CL} 52→} 53→``` 54→

1→# TransformerLens
     2→
     3→<!-- Status Icons -->
     4→[![Pypi](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/transformer-lens?color=blue)](https://pypi.org/project/transformer-lens/)
     5→![Pypi Total Downloads](https://img.shields.io/pepy/dt/transformer_lens?color=blue) ![PyPI -
     6→License](https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/transformer_lens?color=blue) [![Release
     7→CD](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/actions/workflows/release.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/actions/workflows/release.yml)
     8→[![Tests
     9→CD](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/actions/workflows/checks.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/actions/workflows/checks.yml)
    10→[![Docs
    11→CD](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/actions/workflows/pages/pages-build-deployment/badge.svg)](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/actions/workflows/pages/pages-build-deployment)
    12→
    13→A Library for Mechanistic Interpretability of Generative Language Models. Maintained by [Bryce Meyer](https://github.com/bryce13950) and created by [Neel Nanda](https://neelnanda.io/about)
    14→
    15→[![Read the Docs
    16→Here](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Read%20the%20Docs%20Here-blue?style=for-the-badge&logo=Read-the-Docs&logoColor=white&link=https://TransformerLensOrg.github.io/TransformerLens/)](https://TransformerLensOrg.github.io/TransformerLens/)
    17→
    18→This is a library for doing [mechanistic
    19→interpretability](https://distill.pub/2020/circuits/zoom-in/) of GPT-2 Style language models. The
    20→goal of mechanistic interpretability is to take a trained model and reverse engineer the algorithms
    21→the model learned during training from its weights.
    22→
    23→TransformerLens lets you load in 50+ different open source language models, and exposes the internal
    24→activations of the model to you. You can cache any internal activation in the model, and add in
    25→functions to edit, remove or replace these activations as the model runs.
    26→
    27→## Quick Start
    28→
    29→### Install
    30→
    31→```shell
    32→pip install transformer_lens
    33→```
    34→
    35→### Use
    36→
    37→```python
    38→import transformer_lens
    39→
    40→# Load a model (eg GPT-2 Small)
    41→model = transformer_lens.HookedTransformer.from_pretrained("gpt2-small")
    42→
    43→# Run the model and get logits and activations
    44→logits, activations = model.run_with_cache("Hello World")
    45→```
    46→
    47→## Key Tutorials
    48→
    49→* [Introduction to the Library and Mech
    50→  Interp](https://arena-chapter1-transformer-interp.streamlit.app/[1.2]_Intro_to_Mech_Interp)
    51→* [Demo of Main TransformerLens Features](https://neelnanda.io/transformer-lens-demo)
    52→
    53→## Gallery
    54→
    55→Research done involving TransformerLens:
    56→
    57→<!-- If you change this also change docs/source/content/gallery.md -->
    58→* [Progress Measures for Grokking via Mechanistic
    59→  Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.05217) (ICLR Spotlight, 2023) by Neel Nanda, Lawrence
    60→  Chan, Tom Lieberum, Jess Smith, Jacob Steinhardt
    61→* [Finding Neurons in a Haystack: Case Studies with Sparse
    62→  Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.01610) by Wes Gurnee, Neel Nanda, Matthew Pauly, Katherine
    63→  Harvey, Dmitrii Troitskii, Dimitris Bertsimas
    64→* [Towards Automated Circuit Discovery for Mechanistic
    65→  Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.14997) by Arthur Conmy, Augustine N. Mavor-Parker,
    66→  Aengus Lynch, Stefan Heimersheim, Adrià Garriga-Alonso
    67→* [Actually, Othello-GPT Has A Linear Emergent World Representation](https://neelnanda.io/othello)
    68→  by Neel Nanda
    69→* [A circuit for Python docstrings in a 4-layer attention-only
    70→  transformer](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/u6KXXmKFbXfWzoAXn/a-circuit-for-python-docstrings-in-a-4-layer-attention-only)
    71→  by Stefan Heimersheim and Jett Janiak
    72→* [A Toy Model of Universality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.03025) (ICML, 2023) by Bilal Chughtai,
    73→  Lawrence Chan, Neel Nanda
    74→* [N2G: A Scalable Approach for Quantifying Interpretable Neuron Representations in Large Language
    75→  Models](https://openreview.net/forum?id=ZB6bK6MTYq) (2023, ICLR Workshop RTML) by Alex Foote, Neel
    76→  Nanda, Esben Kran, Ioannis Konstas, Fazl Barez
    77→* [Eliciting Latent Predictions from Transformers with the Tuned
    78→  Lens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.08112) by Nora Belrose, Zach Furman, Logan Smith, Danny Halawi,
    79→  Igor Ostrovsky, Lev McKinney, Stella Biderman, Jacob Steinhardt
    80→
    81→User contributed examples of the library being used in action:
    82→
    83→* [Induction Heads Phase Change
    84→  Replication](https://colab.research.google.com/github/ckkissane/induction-heads-transformer-lens/blob/main/Induction_Heads_Phase_Change.ipynb):
    85→  A partial replication of [In-Context Learning and Induction
    86→  Heads](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2022/in-context-learning-and-induction-heads/index.html)
    87→  from Connor Kissane
    88→* [Decision Transformer
    89→  Interpretability](https://github.com/jbloomAus/DecisionTransformerInterpretability): A set of
    90→  scripts for training decision transformers which uses transformer lens to view intermediate
    91→  activations, perform attribution and ablations. A write up of the initial work can be found
    92→  [here](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/bBuBDJBYHt39Q5zZy/decision-transformer-interpretability).
    93→
    94→Check out [our demos folder](https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens/tree/main/demos) for
    95→more examples of TransformerLens in practice
    96→
    97→## Getting Started in Mechanistic Interpretability
    98→
    99→Mechanistic interpretability is a very young and small field, and there are a _lot_ of open
   100→problems. This means there's both a lot of low-hanging fruit, and that the bar for entry is low - if
   101→you would like to help, please try working on one! The standard answer to "why has no one done this
   102→yet" is just that there aren't enough people! Key resources:
   103→
   104→* [A Guide to Getting Started in Mechanistic Interpretability](https://neelnanda.io/getting-started)
   105→* [ARENA Mechanistic Interpretability Tutorials](https://arena3-chapter1-transformer-interp.streamlit.app/) from
   106→  Callum McDougall. A comprehensive practical introduction to mech interp, written in
   107→  TransformerLens - full of snippets to copy and they come with exercises and solutions! Notable
   108→  tutorials:
   109→  * [Coding GPT-2 from
   110→    scratch](https://arena3-chapter1-transformer-interp.streamlit.app/[1.1]_Transformer_from_Scratch), with
   111→    accompanying video tutorial from me ([1](https://neelnanda.io/transformer-tutorial)
   112→    [2](https://neelnanda.io/transformer-tutorial-2)) - a good introduction to transformers
   113→  * [Introduction to Mech Interp and
   114→    TransformerLens](https://arena3-chapter1-transformer-interp.streamlit.app/[1.2]_Intro_to_Mech_Interp): An
   115→    introduction to TransformerLens and mech interp via studying induction heads. Covers the
   116→    foundational concepts of the library
   117→  * [Indirect Object
   118→    Identification](https://arena3-chapter1-transformer-interp.streamlit.app/[1.3]_Indirect_Object_Identification):
   119→    a replication of interpretability in the wild, that covers standard techniques in mech interp
   120→    such as [direct logit
   121→    attribution](https://dynalist.io/d/n2ZWtnoYHrU1s4vnFSAQ519J#z=disz2gTx-jooAcR0a5r8e7LZ),
   122→    [activation patching and path
   123→    patching](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/xh85KbTFhbCz7taD4/how-to-think-about-activation-patching)
   124→* [Mech Interp Paper Reading List](https://neelnanda.io/paper-list)
   125→* [200 Concrete Open Problems in Mechanistic
   126→  Interpretability](https://neelnanda.io/concrete-open-problems)
   127→* [A Comprehensive Mechanistic Interpretability Explainer](https://neelnanda.io/glossary): To look
   128→  up all the jargon and unfamiliar terms you're going to come across!
   129→* [Neel Nanda's Youtube channel](https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCBMJ0D-omcRay8dh4QT0doQ): A range
   130→  of mech interp video content, including [paper
   131→  walkthroughs](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=KV5gbOmHbjU&list=PL7m7hLIqA0hpsJYYhlt1WbHHgdfRLM2eY&index=1),
   132→  and [walkthroughs of doing
   133→  research](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yo4QvDn-vsU&list=PL7m7hLIqA0hr4dVOgjNwP2zjQGVHKeB7T)
   134→
   135→## Support & Community
   136→
   137→[![Contributing
   138→Guide](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Contributing%20Guide-blue?style=for-the-badge&logo=GitHub&logoColor=white)](https://TransformerLensOrg.github.io/TransformerLens/content/contributing.html)
   139→
   140→If you have issues, questions, feature requests or bug reports, please search the issues to check if
   141→it's already been answered, and if not please raise an issue!
   142→
   143→You're also welcome to join the open source mech interp community on
   144→[Slack](https://join.slack.com/t/opensourcemechanistic/shared_invite/zt-2n26nfoh1-TzMHrzyW6HiOsmCESxXtyw).
   145→Please use issues for concrete discussions about the package, and Slack for higher bandwidth
   146→discussions about eg supporting important new use cases, or if you want to make substantial
   147→contributions to the library and want a maintainer's opinion. We'd also love for you to come and
   148→share your projects on the Slack!
   149→
   150→| :exclamation:  HookedSAETransformer Removed   |
   151→|-----------------------------------------------|
   152→
   153→Hooked SAE has been removed from TransformerLens in version 2.0. The functionality is being moved to
   154→[SAELens](http://github.com/jbloomAus/SAELens). For more information on this release, please see the
   155→accompanying
   156→[announcement](https://transformerlensorg.github.io/TransformerLens/content/news/release-2.0.html)
   157→for details on what's new, and the future of TransformerLens.
   158→
   159→## Credits
   160→
   161→This library was created by **[Neel Nanda](https://neelnanda.io)** and is maintained by **[Bryce Meyer](https://github.com/bryce13950)**.
   162→
   163→The core features of TransformerLens were heavily inspired by the interface to [Anthropic's
   164→excellent Garcon tool](https://transformer-circuits.pub/2021/garcon/index.html). Credit to Nelson
   165→Elhage and Chris Olah for building Garcon and showing the value of good infrastructure for enabling
   166→exploratory research!
   167→
   168→### Creator's Note (Neel Nanda)
   169→
   170→I (Neel Nanda) used to work for the [Anthropic interpretability team](transformer-circuits.pub), and
   171→I wrote this library because after I left and tried doing independent research, I got extremely
   172→frustrated by the state of open source tooling. There's a lot of excellent infrastructure like
   173→HuggingFace and DeepSpeed to _use_ or _train_ models, but very little to dig into their internals
   174→and reverse engineer how they work. **This library tries to solve that**, and to make it easy to get
   175→into the field even if you don't work at an industry org with real infrastructure! One of the great
   176→things about mechanistic interpretability is that you don't need large models or tons of compute.
   177→There are lots of important open problems that can be solved with a small model in a Colab notebook!
   178→
   179→### Citation
   180→
   181→Please cite this library as:
   182→
   183→```BibTeX
   184→@misc{nanda2022transformerlens,
   185→    title = {TransformerLens},
   186→    author = {Neel Nanda and Joseph Bloom},
   187→    year = {2022},
   188→    howpublished = {\url{https://github.com/TransformerLensOrg/TransformerLens}},
   189→}
   190→```
   191→

1→# [NeurIPS 2023] Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
     2→
     3→This repo holds the code, demos, and log files for [Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366) by Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Edward Berman, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, Shunyu Yao. 
     4→
     5→![Reflexion RL diagram](./figures/reflexion_rl.png)
     6→
     7→![Reflexion tasks](./figures/reflexion_tasks.png)
     8→
     9→We have released the LeetcodeHardGym [here](https://github.com/GammaTauAI/leetcode-hard-gym)
    10→
    11→## To Run: reasoning (HotPotQA)
    12→
    13→We have provided a set of notebooks to easily run, explore, and interact with the results of the reasoning experiments. Each experiment consists of a random sample of 100 questions from the HotPotQA distractor dataset. Each question in the sample is attempted by an agent with a specific type and reflexion strategy.
    14→
    15→### Setup
    16→
    17→To get started:
    18→
    19→1. Clone this repo and move to the HotPotQA directory:
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→git clone https://github.com/noahshinn/reflexion && cd ./hotpotqa_runs
    23→```
    24→
    25→2. Install the module dependencies into your environment:
    26→
    27→```bash
    28→pip install -r requirements.txt
    29→```
    30→
    31→3. Set `OPENAI_API_KEY` environment variable to your OpenAI API key:
    32→
    33→```bash
    34→export OPENAI_API_KEY=[REDACTED] key>
    35→```
    36→
    37→#### Agent Types
    38→
    39→Agent type is determined by the notebook you choose to run. The available agent types include:
    40→
    41→- `ReAct` - ReAct Agent
    42→
    43→- `CoT_context` - CoT Agent given supporting context about the question 
    44→
    45→- `CoT_no_context` - CoT Agent given no supporting context about the question
    46→
    47→The notebook for each agent type is located in the `./hotpot_runs/notebooks` directory.
    48→
    49→#### Reflexion Strategies
    50→
    51→Each notebook allows you to specify the reflexion strategy to be used by the agents. The available reflexion strategies, which are defined in an `Enum`, include:
    52→
    53→- `ReflexionStrategy.NONE` - The agent is not given any information about its last attempt. 
    54→
    55→- `ReflexionStrategy.LAST_ATTEMPT` - The agent is given its reasoning trace from its last attempt on the question as context.
    56→
    57→- `ReflexionStrategy.REFLEXION` - The agent is given its self-reflection on the last attempt as context. 
    58→
    59→- `ReflexionStrategy.LAST_ATTEMPT_AND_REFLEXION` -  The agent is given both its reasoning trace and self-reflection on the last attempt as context.
    60→
    61→### To Run: decision-making (AlfWorld)
    62→
    63→Clone this repo and move to the AlfWorld directory
    64→
    65→```bash
    66→git clone https://github.com/noahshinn/reflexion && cd ./alfworld_runs
    67→```
    68→
    69→Specify the run parameters in `./run_reflexion.sh`.
    70→`num_trials`: number of iterative learning steps
    71→`num_envs`: number of task-environment pairs per trial
    72→`run_name`: the name for this run
    73→`use_memory`: use persisting memory to store self-reflections (turn off to run a baseline run)
    74→`is_resume`: use logging directory to resume a previous run
    75→`resume_dir`: the logging directory from which to resume the previous run
    76→`start_trial_num`: if resume run, then the trial number of which to start
    77→
    78→Run the trial
    79→
    80→```bash
    81→./run_reflexion.sh
    82→```
    83→
    84→The logs will be sent to `./root/<run_name>`.
    85→
    86→### Another Note
    87→
    88→Due to the nature of these experiments, it may not be feasible for individual developers to rerun the results as GPT-4 has limited access and significant API charges. All runs from the paper and additional results are logged in `./alfworld_runs/root` for decision-making, `./hotpotqa_runs/root` for reasoning, and `./programming_runs/root` for programming
    89→
    90→### Other Notes
    91→
    92→Check out the original implementation [here](https://github.com/noahshinn/reflexion-draft)
    93→
    94→Read one of the original blog posts [here](https://nanothoughts.substack.com/p/reflecting-on-reflexion)
    95→
    96→Check out an [Appl](https://github.com/appl-team/appl) implementation [here](https://github.com/appl-team/reppl/tree/main/reflexion).
    97→
    98→Check out an interesting type-prediction implementation here: [OpenTau](https://github.com/GammaTauAI/opentau)
    99→
   100→For all questions, contact [noahrshinn@gmail.com](noahrshinn@gmail.com)
   101→
   102→### Cite
   103→
   104→```bibtex
   105→@misc{shinn2023reflexion,
   106→      title={Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning}, 
   107→      author={Noah Shinn and Federico Cassano and Edward Berman and Ashwin Gopinath and Karthik Narasimhan and Shunyu Yao},
   108→      year={2023},
   109→      eprint={2303.11366},
   110→      archivePrefix={arXiv},
   111→      primaryClass={cs.AI}
   112→}
   113→```
   114→

Based on the search results, I found several relevant GitHub repositories related to probing self-reflection in language models. Here are the key findings:

## Self-Reflection in Language Models Repositories

Several repositories focus on self-reflection capabilities in LLMs:

1. **Reflexion** - This repo holds the code, demos, and log files for Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning by Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Edward Berman, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, Shunyu Yao.

2. **SelfReflect (Apple)** - This software project accompanies the research paper SelfReflect: Can LLMs Communicate Their Internal Answer Distribution? The SelfReflect metric, an information-theoretic distance between a given summary and a distribution over answers, was developed to test whether LLMs possess this capability.

3. **LLM-SelfReflection-Eval** - This code is associated with the NAACL 2024 Findings paper: "When Hindsight is Not 20/20: Testing Limits on Reflective Thinking in Large Language Models".

4. **Self-Reflection in LLM Agents** - This study investigated the effects of self-reflection in large language models (LLMs) on problem-solving performance by instructing nine popular LLMs to answer a series of multiple-choice questions to provide a performance baseline.

However, I did not find a specific repository explicitly named "ProbingReflection" in the search results. The results show various related projects on self-reflection and probing language models, but not one with that exact name. You may want to search more specifically or check if the repository name has changed or uses a different naming convention.

Based on the search results, I found several relevant GitHub repositories that combine concepts of probing, self-critique, and internal representations in language models:

## Key Repositories Found:

1. **ICSFSurvey** (IAAR-Shanghai): A survey repository titled "Internal Consistency and Self-Feedback in Large Language Models: A Survey" that provides a unified perspective on the self-evaluation and self-updating mechanisms in LLMs, encapsulated within the frameworks of Internal Consistency and Self-Feedback.

2. **Othello World** (likenneth): A repository providing code for training, probing and intervening the Othello-GPT to explore emergent world representations in sequence models. The research uncovers evidence of an emergent nonlinear internal representation of the board state.

3. **LLM Interpretability Resources**: Multiple curated lists exist including:
   - JShollaj/awesome-llm-interpretability - a curated list of Large Language Model (LLM) Interpretability resources
   - ruizheliUOA/Awesome-Interpretability-in-Large-Language-Models collecting relevant resources about interpretability in LLMs

4. **Self-Correction Repositories**:
   - teacherpeterpan/self-correction-llm-papers - a collection of research papers for Self-Correcting Large Language Models with Automated Feedback
   - CRITIC: Large Language Models Can Self-Correct with Tool-Interactive Critiquing by Zhibin Gou et al., published at ICLR 2024

These repositories cover the intersection of probing techniques, self-critique mechanisms, and analysis of internal representations in language models.

Based on the web search results, I found several relevant GitHub repositories related to your query about representation drift, self-reflection, and probing LLM internal states:

## Key Findings:

**Representation Drift & Internal States Probing:**

One project enables tracing logic errors or hallucinations back to earlier internal states and inspecting where the drift began, with the ability to compare faulty paths with corrected ones and compute differences in internal representation.

**Chess LLM Interpretability:**
A repository on chess LLM interpretability visualizes the internal board state of a GPT trained on chess PGN strings, evaluating LLMs through linear probes to check the LLMs' internal understanding of board state and ability to estimate player skill level.

**Representation Stability:**
The CCPS method offers a novel approach to estimate LLM confidence by analyzing the stability of internal representations when subjected to targeted adversarial perturbations, extracting features reflecting the model's response to these perturbations and training a lightweight classifier to predict answer correctness.

**Self-Reflection Research:**
A survey project includes "Reflection-Bench: probing AI intelligence with reflection" and research on internal consistency and self-feedback strategies.

**LLM Metacognition:**
One project proposes a paradigm shift from machine learning to "machine self-improvement through reflective latent simulation," where a metacognitive framework enables improvement through self-reflection and rehearsal, with the transformer becoming a cognitive substrate whose thoughts can be saved, explored, and optimized.

The repositories range from theoretical frameworks to practical implementations for probing, interpreting, and improving LLM internal representations through reflection mechanisms.

1→# Self-Reflection in LLM Agents: Effects on Problem-Solving Performance
     2→
     3→## Abstract
     4→In this study, we investigated the effects of self-reflection in large language models (LLMs) on problem-solving performance. We instructed nine popular LLMs to answer a series of multiple-choice questions to provide a performance baseline. Then, for each incorrectly answered question, we instructed eight types of self-reflecting LLM agents to reflect on their mistakes and provide themselves with guidance to improve problem-solving. Then, using this guidance, each self-reflecting agent attempted to re-answer the same questions again. Our results indicate that LLM agents are able to significantly improve their problem-solving performance through self-reflection. In addition, we compared the various types of self-reflection to determine their individual contribution to performance. 
     5→
     6→## Documents
     7→ - [Research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06682)
     8→ - [Presentation video](https://youtu.be/VCPwYAQTcpE)
     9→ - [Presentation slides](https://matthewrenze.com/wp-content/uploads/presentations/self-reflection.pdf)
    10→ - [Research homepage](https://matthewrenze.com/research/self-reflection-in-llm-agents/)
    11→
    12→## Code
    13→1. [Solve with Baseline](source/1_solve_with_baseline.py) - answers all questions using the baseline agent
    14→2. [Reflect on Solution](source/2_reflect_on_solutions.py) - self-reflects on incorrectly answered problems given the correct answer
    15→3. [Save Reflections](source/3_save_reflections.py) - separates reflections by type, redacts answers, and saves reflection text
    16→4. [Solve with Reflection](source/4_solve_with_reflections.py) - re-answers all incorrectly answered questions using the reflections
    17→5. [Plot Accuracy](source/5_plot_accuracy.py) - plots the accuracy for each agent
    18→6. [Plot Accuracy by Model and Agent](source/6_plot_accuracy_by_model_and_agent.py) - plots the accuracy by model and agent
    19→7. [Plot Accuracy by Exam and Agent](source/7_plot_accuracy_by_exam_and_agent.py) - plots the accuracy for each model by exam and agent
    20→8. [Analyze Details](source/8_analyze_details.py) - performs the McNemar test and creates a table of the results
    21→9. [Analyze Keywords](source/9_analyze_keywords.py) - analyzes the error keywords produced by the self-reflections
    22→
    23→## Data
    24→ - [Details](data/details/) - the low-level level details for each question answered in CSV format
    25→ - [Dialogs](data/dialogs/) - the dialog for each question answered in JSON format
    26→ - [Exams](data/exams/) - the exams containing MCQA problems in JSONL format
    27→ - [Logs](data/logs/) - the log files for each question answered and self-reflection in plain-text format
    28→ - [Plots](data/plots/) - the data visualizations of the results in PDF format
    29→ - [Reflections](data/reflections/) - the text generated during the self-reflections process stored as plain text files
    30→ - [Results](data/results/) - the results from the experiment in CSV format
    31→ - [Tables](data/tables/) - the tabular results of the analysis stored as CSV files
    32→
    33→
